questions.question:
Let p be a pointer as shown in the figure in a single linked list (p points to a node, and there are at least two more nodes after it).

What do the following assignment statements achieve?

q := p->next
p->next := q->next
q->next := (q->next)->next
(p->next)->next := q
questions.answer: These statements swap the two nodes immediately following p, i.e., if the list is ...->p->A->B->C->..., after execution the list becomes ...->p->B->A->C->...
questions.to_find: What transformation is achieved by the given sequence of pointer assignments on a singly linked list?

understand.plain: We have a singly linked list and a pointer p. The four assignment statements manipulate the next pointers of the nodes that follow p. The goal is to figure out the net effect on the list structure after all four statements execute in order.

keywords.explain: A linear data structure where each node contains data and a single pointer (next) to the subsequent node.
Changing the next pointers of nodes to rearrange the order of nodes in the list without copying data.
keywords.example: If p->next = B and we set p->next = C, then B is detached from p's chain.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 600 200' width='600' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>p</text><rect x='130' y='30' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>A</text><rect x='250' y='30' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>B</text><rect x='370' y='30' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='410' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>C</text><line x1='90' y1='50' x2='130' y2='50' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='210' y1='50' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='330' y1='50' x2='370' y2='50' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='300' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Before: p -> A -> B -> C</text><text x='300' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>After:  p -> B -> A -> C</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Before: p -> A -> B -> C. After executing the four statements: p -> B -> A -> C. The two nodes A and B (immediately following p) are swapped.

given.aim: Determine the net effect of the four pointer assignment statements on the singly linked list.

terms.meaning: A pointer to some node in the singly linked list, with at least two nodes following it.
terms.example: p points to node X, X->next = A, A->next = B
terms.connects: p is the anchor; all manipulations are relative to the nodes after p.

terms.meaning: q is set to point to the node immediately after p (call it A).
terms.example: If p->A->B->C, then q now points to A.
terms.connects: q acts as a temporary pointer to save the reference to A before relinking.

terms.meaning: A = original p->next, B = original p->next->next; the two nodes to be swapped.
terms.connects: The four statements collectively swap A and B in-place.

given.plan: Trace through each assignment one at a time, tracking what each pointer points to, and observe the final configuration of next pointers.

steps.title: Initial state and step 1: save q = A
steps.apply: Let A = p->next, B = A->next, C = B->next. After step 1: q = A. State: p->A->B->C, q=A.
steps.note: q is a temporary copy of the pointer to A; we must save it before relinking.

steps.title: Step 2: p->next skips A, points to B
steps.apply: p->next = q->next = A->next = B. State: p->B->C (A is disconnected from p's chain), q=A.
steps.note: Now p directly points to B, bypassing A.

steps.title: Step 3: B->next skips B's old next, points to C
steps.apply: q->next is currently A->next. But wait: q=A and A->next was B, which hasn't changed yet on A. So q->next = A->next = B. (q->next)->next = B->next = C. So A->next := C. State: p->B->C, A->C, q=A.
steps.note: This sets A's next to C (skipping B), preparing A to be inserted after B.

steps.title: Step 4: B->next is set to A
steps.apply: p->next is now B. So B->next := q = A. Final state: p->B->A->C.
steps.note: The insertion is complete. The two nodes A and B have been swapped.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply:
Original order: p -> A -> B -> C -> ...
Final order:    p -> B -> A -> C -> ...
The two nodes immediately after p have been swapped.
steps.note: This is a classic in-place swap of two adjacent nodes in a singly linked list using a temporary pointer.

solution.result: The four statements swap the two nodes immediately following p. If the list was p -> A -> B -> C, it becomes p -> B -> A -> C.

questions.formula_note: In-place adjacent node swap in singly linked list using a temporary pointer; requires saving the first node in q before re-linking.

questions.question: Write a constant time algorithm to insert a node with data d just before the node with address p of a singly linked list.
questions.answer: Copy the data of node p into a new node, insert the new node after p, then overwrite p's data with d. This achieves O(1) insertion 'before' p without traversing from the head.
questions.to_find: A constant-time O(1) algorithm to insert a new node with value d just before node p in a singly linked list (given only the pointer p, not the predecessor).

understand.plain: In a singly linked list, inserting before a given node p normally requires finding p's predecessor by traversing from the head, which takes O(n) time. The trick to achieve O(1) is to insert a new node AFTER p and then swap the data between p and the new node, making it appear as if a new node was inserted before p.

keywords.explain: An algorithm that takes the same number of steps regardless of the size of the input.
keywords.example: Accessing an array element by index is O(1).

keywords.explain: Instead of re-linking to insert before p (which requires the predecessor), insert after p and swap data values to achieve the same logical effect.
keywords.example: If p has data 5 and we want to insert 3 before it: create node after p with data 5, then set p->data = 3. Result: ...->3->5->... which is the same as inserting 3 before the original 5 node.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 620 200' width='620' lbl'>p:d</text><rect x='250' y='130' width='80' height='40' class='ok'/><text x='290' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>new:X</text><rect x='370' y='130' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='410' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>next</text><line x1='90' y1='150' x2='130' y2='150' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='210' y1='150' x2='250' y2='150' understand.visual_alt: Before: prev -> p(X) -> next. After: prev -> p(d) -> new(X) -> next. The new node with value d appears before the original data X in the list, effectively inserting d before X.

given.aim: Insert a node with data d just before node p in O(1) time, given only the pointer p.

terms.meaning: Pointer to the node before which we want to insert new data d.
terms.example: p points to node containing value X in the list.
terms.connects: We cannot find p's predecessor in O(1) since it's a singly linked list; we must work around this.

terms.meaning: The data value to be inserted as a new node just before p.
terms.example: If list is 1->5->9 and p points to 5, insert d=3 to get 1->3->5->9.
terms.connects: d goes into p's position logically; p's old data moves to a new node after p.

terms.meaning: An allocated node that stores p's current data and is linked after p.
terms.example: new->data = p->data; new->next = p->next; p->next = new;
terms.connects: After relinking, setting p->data = d completes the logical insertion before p.

given.plan: Allocate a new node, copy p's data into it, link the new node after p (adjusting next pointers), then overwrite p->data with d. This runs in O(1) as no traversal is needed.

steps.title: Allocate a new node and copy p's data
steps.apply: Create a new node. Set new->data = p->data (copy p's current value). Set new->next = p->next (point new node to p's successor).
steps.note: We preserve p's original data and linkage in the new node.

steps.title: Insert new node after p
steps.apply: Set p->next = new. Now the list looks like: ...->p(old_data)->new(old_data)->original_next->...
steps.note: The new node is now immediately after p in the list.

steps.title: Overwrite p's data with d
steps.apply: Set p->data = d. Now p holds the new value d and the new node holds the old value. List: ...->p(d)->new(old_data)->...
steps.note: Logically, d has been inserted 'before' p's old data without traversing from the head.

steps.title: Verify O(1) complexity
steps.apply: Steps: 1 malloc, 3 pointer assignments, 2 data assignments. All are constant-time operations with no loops or recursion. Total: O(1).
steps.note: This trick works because we exploit the fact that we can insert after a given node in O(1), and data swapping achieves the logical 'insert before' effect.

solution.result: Algorithm: (1) new = malloc(node); new->data = p->data; new->next = p->next; (2) p->next = new; (3) p->data = d. This inserts d before p's original data in O(1) time.

questions.formula_note: The insert-before-p in O(1) trick: insert after p and swap data, exploiting that 'insert after' needs no predecessor traversal.

questions.question:
In the worst case, the number of comparisons needed to search a single linked list of length n for a given element is

A. log_2(n)
B. n/2
C. log_2(n) - 1
D. n
questions.to_find: The worst-case number of comparisons to search a singly linked list of length n for a given element.

understand.plain: Searching a singly linked list requires starting from the head and examining each node one by one. In the worst case, the element is either the last node or not present at all, requiring us to compare with every single element in the list.

keywords.explain: Sequential search from the first element to the last; the only search method available for a singly linked list (no random access, no binary search possible).
keywords.example: To find 7 in 1->3->5->7->9, we compare 1, 3, 5, 7 (4 comparisons).

keywords.explain: The scenario that requires the maximum number of operations; for search, it is when the element is at the last position or absent.
keywords.example: Searching for 9 in 1->3->5->7->9 requires n=5 comparisons.

understand.visual_alt: A singly linked list of n nodes. In the worst case, we must traverse all n nodes before finding the element at position n or determining it is absent.

given.aim: Find the worst-case comparison count for searching a singly linked list of length n.

terms.meaning: The number of nodes (length) in the singly linked list.
terms.example: n = 5 means the list has 5 nodes.
terms.connects: The worst case is directly proportional to n.

terms.meaning: Checking whether the current node's data equals the target search key.
terms.example: At each node, one comparison: if (node->data == key).
terms.connects: Each node visited costs exactly one comparison.

terms.meaning: The target element is the last node, or it is not present in the list.
terms.example: Search for 10 in list 1->2->...->n: compare all n nodes before determining absence.
terms.connects: In this case, all n comparisons are made.

given.plan: A singly linked list does not support binary search (no random access). The only option is linear search from head to tail. In the worst case, all n elements must be compared.

steps.title: Identify the search method for singly linked list
steps.apply: Unlike arrays, a singly linked list does not allow random access (no indexing). To reach node k, you must traverse all k-1 preceding nodes. Therefore, binary search is not possible.

steps.title: Determine worst case
steps.apply: In the worst case, the search element is the last node in the list, OR the element is not in the list at all. Either way, all n nodes are visited and compared. So worst case = n comparisons.
steps.note: log_2(n) would apply to binary search on a sorted array; n/2 is the average case for linear search; log_2(n)-1 has no basis here.

solution.result: The worst-case number of comparisons is n. Answer: D. n

questions.formula_note: Linear search on singly linked list: O(n) worst case because no random access means binary search is impossible.

questions.answer: B. the elements in the list are sorted in non-decreasing order of data value
questions.to_find: The condition under which the recursive function f returns 1 (true) for a linked list.

understand.plain: The function f recursively checks a condition on the linked list. It returns 1 if: the list is NULL (empty), or has only one node, or the current node's data is less than or equal to the next node's data AND the rest of the list also satisfies this property. This is exactly the definition of a sorted list in non-decreasing order.

keywords.explain: In C, || returns true immediately if the first operand is true, without evaluating the rest.
keywords.example: (p == NULL) || ... : if p is NULL, the function returns 1 immediately without checking p->next.

keywords.explain: Each element is >= the previous element; duplicates are allowed.

understand.visual_alt: A linked list 1->2->3->4. The function checks at each node whether data[i] <= data[i+1]. If all consecutive pairs satisfy this, f returns 1, meaning the list is sorted in non-decreasing order.

given.aim: Determine what condition on the linked list makes the function f return 1.

terms.meaning: Base case: empty list; f returns 1 (vacuously true).
terms.example: An empty list is trivially sorted.

terms.meaning: Base case: single-element list; f returns 1 (a single element is always sorted).
terms.example: List with one node: 5 -> NULL; f returns 1.
terms.connects: Handles the single-node base case for recursion.

terms.meaning: The current node's data is less than or equal to the next node's data.
terms.example: If current = 3 and next = 5, then 3 <= 5 is true.
terms.connects: This is the non-decreasing condition between adjacent nodes.

given.plan: Trace the logic of the recursive function: it returns 1 only when every consecutive pair of nodes satisfies data[i] <= data[i+1], which is the definition of a non-decreasingly sorted list.

steps.title: Analyze the base cases
steps.formula_raw: f(p) = 1 if p == NULL or p->next == NULL
steps.apply: If the list is empty (p == NULL) or has exactly one node (p->next == NULL), f returns 1 immediately. These are trivially sorted lists.
steps.note: Short-circuit OR means these are checked first; the recursive call only happens for lists with 2+ nodes.

steps.title: Analyze the recursive case
steps.apply: For a list of 2+ nodes, f returns 1 if AND ONLY IF: (1) the current node's data <= next node's data, AND (2) the rest of the list starting at p->next also satisfies f. This checks ALL consecutive pairs in the list recursively.
steps.note: Both conditions must hold (&&); if any consecutive pair has data[i] > data[i+1], f returns 0.

steps.title: Identify the overall condition
steps.apply:
The function returns 1 if for every node i: data[i] <= data[i+1]. This is precisely the condition that the list is sorted in non-decreasing order. Examples:
- 1->2->3->4: 1<=2, 2<=3, 3<=4 -> f=1
- 1->2->5->3: 1<=2, 2<=5, but 5>3 -> f=0
- 3->3->3: 3<=3, 3<=3 -> f=1 (non-decreasing allows duplicates)
steps.note: Option C (non-increasing) would require >=; Option A misses multi-element sorted lists; Option D is incorrect.

solution.result: f returns 1 if and only if the elements in the list are sorted in non-decreasing order of data value. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Recursive linked list traversal checking adjacent node ordering; the && of (data[i] <= data[i+1]) and f(p->next) collectively verifies non-decreasing sorted order.
